hero.tagline: Kansas small claims court offers a streamlined process for resolving disputes up to $10,000 without attorney representation.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Lawyers at trial
at_a_glance.detail: Parties must represent themselves unless the opposing party is an attorney

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in district court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

annual_filing_cap.notes: No person can file more than 20 small claims cases in one calendar year.

limits.split_claims_explanation: If a claim exceeds the $10,000 cap, you must either waive the excess amount to stay in small claims court or file in a higher court. Splitting one dispute into multiple claims to fit under the cap is not allowed.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Kansas allows you to file claims related to breaches of contract and money owed, with a statute of limitations of 5 years for written contracts and 3 years for oral contracts.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $2,000, and they failed to repay as promised.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement, such as emails or witness testimony.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $1,500 with the understanding they'd repay in six months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring any written agreement or proof of the loan and attempts to collect.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note for $3,000 and failed to make the agreed payments.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $2,500 worth of consulting services, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Bring the invoice, proof of service delivery, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $1,200 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $800 in unpaid rent for the past two months.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $500 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: After a 30-day notice, you may recover the check amount plus damages. Provide the bounced check and notice sent.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $30 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $4,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the loan agreement and proof of payments made on behalf of the borrower.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Kansas allows consumers to file claims related to defective products and services, with a statute of limitations of 3 years for most cases.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,000 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Bring the purchase receipt, warranty, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: A cleaning service charged $600 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement and evidence of incomplete work.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged $2,000 for repairs that didn't fix the issue, and refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices and evidence of unresolved issues.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $5,000 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver services
claims.example: You paid $1,200 for a catering service that never showed up for your event.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from service date. Bring the service agreement and proof of payment.

claims.name: Unauthorized charges after subscription cancellation
claims.example: You canceled a $50/month subscription, but the company continued to charge your account.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation and bank statements.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $7,000 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Bring the bill of sale and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product for $500 with features it didn't have, leading you to purchase under false pretenses.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from purchase. Provide the advertisement and proof of purchase.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Kansas allows claims for property damage with a statute of limitations of 2 years from the date of damage.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver caused $3,000 in damage to your car and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from accident date. Bring the police report and repair estimates.

claims.name: Neighbor damaged your property
claims.example: A neighbor's tree fell on your fence, causing $1,500 in damage.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos and repair estimates.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left $2,000 worth of damage to your rental unit.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery of damage. Bring the lease agreement and photos of damage.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog dug up your garden, causing $800 in damage.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from damage date. Provide photos and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left a $1,200 bicycle with a friend, and they returned it damaged.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery of damage. Provide evidence of the agreement and photos of damage.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Kansas allows claims related to landlord-tenant disputes, with specific statutes governing security deposits and other issues.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,000 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: Landlord must return deposit or provide itemized deductions within 14 days. Bring the lease agreement and move-out inspection report.

claims.name: Failure to provide itemized deductions from deposit
claims.example: Landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: Landlord must provide itemized deductions within 14 days. Bring the lease agreement and correspondence with the landlord.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had no heating during winter, and the landlord refused to fix it.
claims.notes: Landlord must maintain habitable conditions. Bring photos and records of communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord wrongfully retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, the landlord kept your personal belongings worth $2,000.
claims.notes: Landlord must return tenant's property. Provide inventory of items and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Landlord conducted illegal eviction
claims.example: Landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: Self-help evictions are illegal. Bring evidence of the eviction and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent after lease termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but the landlord didn't return the last month's rent after early termination.
claims.notes: Landlord must return unearned rent. Provide the lease agreement and proof of payments.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $1,200 in unpaid rent for the past three months.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement and records of unpaid rent.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You paid $500 to fix a leaking roof after the landlord refused, and deducted it from rent.
claims.notes: Tenant may repair and deduct if landlord fails to act. Provide repair receipts and communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Kansas allows claims related to unpaid wages and final paychecks, with specific statutes governing these issues.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $1,000 for hours worked but hasn't paid.
claims.notes: Employer must pay wages due. Bring pay stubs and records of hours worked.

claims.name: Employer withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer didn't provide your final paycheck of $800.
claims.notes: Final wages due upon termination. Provide resignation letter and records of unpaid wages.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned commissions
claims.example: You earned $2,000 in commissions, but your employer hasn't paid them.
claims.notes: Commissions are considered wages. Bring commission agreements and sales records.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't paid the additional $300 owed.
claims.notes: Overtime must be paid as per agreement. Provide timesheets and pay records.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Kansas allows claims for minor personal injuries and property disputes, with specific statutes governing these issues.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from injury date. Bring medical records and incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You loaned a friend a $500 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from demand for return. Provide evidence of the loan and demand for return.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: Someone stole your bicycle worth $800, and you know their identity.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from theft date. Provide police reports and evidence of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Kansas allows claims related to vehicle repairs and wrongful towing, with specific statutes governing these issues.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged $1,500 for repairs that didn't fix the problem.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices and evidence of unresolved issues.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, resulting in $300 in fees.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from towing date. Provide towing receipts and photos of the parking area.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are not handled in small claims court and must be filed in the appropriate division of the district court.
Disputes involving the title to real estate are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims involving defamation are not suitable for small claims court due to their complexity.
Professional malpractice claims exceed the jurisdictional limits and complexity suitable for small claims court.
Matters such as divorce, child custody, and probate are not handled in small claims court.
Small claims court does not have jurisdiction over claims against federal entities.
Workers' compensation claims are handled by the Kansas Department of Labor, not small claims court.
Class action lawsuits are beyond the scope of small claims court and must be filed in a higher court.
Small claims court cannot issue injunctions or orders for specific performance; it can only award monetary damages.
Bankruptcy cases are handled exclusively by federal bankruptcy courts, not small claims court.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in small claims court; only actual damages are recoverable.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $30 + civil penalty up to $500 OR 3x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees are generally not recoverable in small claims court.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Division of the District Court

filing_options: Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially incurring additional costs and delays.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing availability varies by county; check with the local district court clerk.

frequent_filer.notes: No person can file more than 20 small claims cases in one calendar year.

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service fees.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fee is added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention and serves as evidence of your attempt to settle.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad checks, a 30-day written notice is required before pursuing statutory damages.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: No specific pre-filing demand required for wage claims, but documenting requests for payment is advisable.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of deductions from the security deposit within 14 days; failure to do so may entitle the tenant to recover the full deposit.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: No specific pre-filing requirements for consumer protection claims, but documenting complaints and attempts to resolve is beneficial.

forms.name: Small Claims Petition
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's claim against the defendant.

forms.name: Summons
forms.description: Notifies the defendant of the lawsuit and the date of the hearing.

forms.name: Answer
forms.description: Allows the defendant to respond to the plaintiff's claims.

forms.name: Counterclaim
forms.description: Filed by the defendant if they have a claim against the plaintiff.

forms.name: Affidavit of Indigency
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees due to financial hardship.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Filed to appeal the court's decision to a higher court.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: Sheriff or constable personally hands the papers to the defendant.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: Clerk sends the summons and petition via certified mail to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A licensed individual serves the papers to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Kansas statutes and the laws of the state where the defendant resides.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court permission for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendant is not required to file a written answer but must appear at the hearing; failure to appear may result in a default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may grant a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim.

motion_to_vacate_form.name: Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment

response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: If the motion to vacate is denied, the defendant may appeal within 14 days.

counterclaim.transfer_to_higher_court_notes: If the counterclaim exceeds the small claims limit, the case may be transferred to a higher court.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Attorneys are not permitted to represent parties in small claims court unless the opposing party is an attorney.
hearing.format: Bench trial with informal procedures; each party presents their case directly to the judge.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter through the court clerk's office well in advance of the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks.
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some courts may offer mediation services; check with the local district court.

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in the district court.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed within 14 days.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on unpaid judgments at a rate of 10% per annum.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and transfers funds to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor maintains sufficient funds in bank accounts.
exemptions: Certain amounts may be exempt under state law

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File the judgment with the county recorder to place a lien on the debtor's property, which must be satisfied upon sale or refinancing.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real property; may take time to realize payment.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Seize and sell debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ from the court, and the sheriff seizes and sells the debtor's non-exempt property at auction.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt property.
exemptions: Certain personal property may be exempt under state law

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to revive the judgment before expiration; if granted, the judgment is extended for another 5 years.
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy may discharge certain judgments; consult legal counsel for specifics.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order of filing liens.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can represent themselves in small claims court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name must file a Certificate of Assumed Name with the Secretary of State.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may authorize an employee to appear on their behalf.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may face challenges in enforcing contracts; consult legal counsel.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the claim limit
pitfalls.what_happens: If your claim exceeds $10,000, the court may dismiss the case or require you to waive the excess amount.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure your claim, excluding interest and costs, does not exceed the $10,000 limit.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: Failure to properly serve the defendant can result in delays or dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Kansas statutes for service of process and consider using the sheriff's office or a professional process server.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will result in dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type before filing.

pitfalls.title: Failing to collect judgment
pitfalls.what_happens: Winning a judgment does not guarantee payment; you may need to take additional steps to collect.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Familiarize yourself with post-judgment collection methods such as garnishment and liens.

pitfalls.title: Not bringing necessary evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Lack of evidence can lead to losing your case, even if you're in the right.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Bring all relevant documents, receipts, contracts, and witness information to court.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring counterclaims
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant files a counterclaim and you are unprepared, you could lose money.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Be prepared to defend against potential counterclaims by gathering evidence and understanding your rights.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, attorneys are not permitted to represent parties in Kansas small claims court unless the opposing party is an attorney.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: Most small claims cases in Kansas are resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing to judgment.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Kansas small claims court is $10,000, excluding interest and court costs.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may grant a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims judgment within 14 days by filing a notice of appeal and paying the required fee.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Kansas is valid for 5 years and can be renewed before expiration to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Kansas small claims court does not allow attorney representation unless the opposing party is an attorney.
The maximum claim amount in Kansas small claims court is $10,000, excluding interest and court costs.
Plaintiffs are limited to filing no more than 20 small claims cases in a calendar year.
Eviction cases are not handled in small claims court and must be filed in the appropriate division of the district court.
Winning a judgment does not guarantee payment; plaintiffs may need to pursue collection methods such as garnishment or liens.

interesting_findings: Kansas small claims court provides a simplified and informal process for resolving disputes without the need for attorney representation.
The court's jurisdiction is limited to claims not exceeding $10,000, promoting efficient resolution of smaller disputes.
Plaintiffs should be aware of the statute of limitations applicable to their specific claim type to ensure timely filing.

sources.label: Kan. Stat. Chapter 61 Art. 27 (Small Claims Procedure Act)
Kan. Stat. Chapter 60 (Code of Civil Procedure)
Kan. Stat. § 60-501 et seq. (Statute of limitations)
Kansas Rules of the Supreme Court — Civil
Kan. Stat. § 61-2706 (Claims exceeding small claims jurisdiction)
Kan. Stat. § 60-303 et seq. (Service of process)
Kan. Stat. § 50-623 et seq. (Consumer Protection Act)
